title: Systems Awareness

content.statement: Everything is a system with inputs, outputs, and feedback.
content.question: Which part of your life are you still treating as a list of tasks instead of a system?
content.equation: Output Score = (Input Quality plus Structure Quality plus Feedback Speed) divided by 3, each rated 1-10. The original 'Output = f(Inputs, Structure, Feedback)' is function notation, not something you can literally calculate.
content.mental_model: Zoom out: see the whole mechanism, not one part.
content.business_lens: Business = interconnected flows (marketing → sales → ops → retention).
content.life_lens: Life = interconnected systems (sleep → energy → work → money).
sop: Pick one recurring problem area and name it explicitly as a system, not a task list.
List every part or component involved and how they connect to each other.
Rate the Input Quality, Structure Quality, and Feedback Speed of that system, each 1-10 (see calculation, page 6).
Identify which of those three scores is lowest, that's where to intervene first.
Re-check the score in 30 days to confirm the change actually moved the system, not just the symptom.
questions: Which part of your life are you still treating as a list of tasks instead of a system?
Pick one recurring problem, what are its actual inputs, and which one are you never touching?
If you mapped the feedback loop in your business right now, would you even know where the signal comes back in?

definitions.def: When a system's output is fed back as input, allowing self-correction or self-amplification.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Input Quality 7, Structure Quality 5, Feedback Speed 8. (7 plus 5 plus 8) divided by 3 equals 6.7 out of 10. Higher means your system is well-designed enough to produce good outputs reliably; lower means at least one part, inputs, structure, or feedback, is dragging the whole system down.
content.closing_statement: See the mechanism, not just the moving parts.

caption:
Principle Nº 101: Systems Awareness

Everything is a system with inputs, outputs, and feedback.

Ask yourself: Which part of your life are you still treating as a list of tasks instead of a system?
